text: The surgeon identifies the right round ligament and applies a ligature device to secure it.

The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks during the hysterectomy, noting the round ligament located four centimeters lateral to the uterus, the fallopian tube three centimeters, and the ovarian ligament two centimeters from the uterus.
The surgeon uses an electrosurgical device to coagulate the ovarian ligament, following the previous coagulation of the fallopian tube.

The surgeon positions the uterine manipulator towards the contralateral shoulder, ensuring it is neither antiverted nor retroverted. The assistant helps maintain this position while the surgeon prepares to take the left side of the upper pedicles. The round ligament is identified approximately four centimeters lateral to the uterus.
The surgeon uses a ligature to secure the fallopian tube approximately three centimeters lateral to the uterus, following the dissection of the right round ligament.
The surgeon isolates and suspends the ovarian ligament, preparing it for further surgical manipulation.
The surgical setup includes two ipsilateral ports and one contralateral port. The surgeon identifies the conular side and positions instruments 2.5 centimeters over the broad ligament.
The surgeon retroverts the uterus and stretches the anterior leaf of the broad ligament using the left hand, manipulating the peritoneum in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses the right hand to cut the anterior leaf of the broad ligament while the uterine manipulator moves the uterus to a retroverted position at five, six, and seven o'clock. This maneuver helps to separate the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, ensuring the ureter is moved away for safety. Following this, the surgeon begins to separate the bladder.
The surgeon uses a plagelet to gently separate the bladder from surrounding tissues, noting that the process is facilitated by the patient's history of previous vaginal deliveries.
The surgeon separates the bladder from the vagina, revealing a crisscross pattern of vessels that is clearly visible.
The surgeon separates the bladder from the surrounding tissue, using a peanut sponge if necessary to aid in the dissection.
The surgeon separates the bladder pillar, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon retroverts the uterus to facilitate the separation of the bladder from the uterus, ensuring clear visibility and access to the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon maintains the uterus in a retroverted position to facilitate the separation of the bladder. The bladder pillar is visible and is being separated on either side.
The surgeon observes the ureter moving laterally, revealing a thin pedicle of the uterine structure, highlighting its importance in the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the separation of the bladder pillar on the right side and proceeds to separate the bladder pillar on the left side.
The surgeon retroverts the uterus, completing the separation of the bladder pillars on both sides.
The surgeon stretches the posterior peritoneum with one hand while using either a harmonic scalpel or monopolar cautery to cut through the posterior peritoneum.
The surgeon carefully cuts the posterior peritoneum above the arc of the uterosacral ligament, ensuring precise incision placement to avoid damage to surrounding structures.
The video shows the uterine hump on the left side, with the uterus positioned at one o'clock. The surrounding tissue, referred to as the plutonium, is visible.

The surgeon moves the uterus to an eleven o'clock position to facilitate cutting the right side of the posterior peritoneum. The uterus is introverted, and the posterior peritoneum is being separated on the right side.
The surgeon directs the cutting instrument towards the upper arc of the uterosacral ligament, ensuring precise alignment for the dissection of the posterior peritoneum.

The surgeon stretches the peritoneum before cutting to avoid contact with the vein, which is positioned posteriorly to the artery. This precaution is taken to prevent severe bleeding from the vein during the procedure.
The surgeon has separated the uterine hump on the right side, which is clearly visible. This separation aids in safely moving the ureter away from the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the previous step and proceeds to address the uterine pedicle, preparing for further dissection or ligation.

The surgeon carefully separates the bladder from the uterus, opening the anterior and posterior leaf of the broad ligament. The uterine pedicle is visible, and the surgeon prepares to use a ligature for further dissection.

The surgeon coagulates the ascending part of the uterine artery, focusing on the anterior section before reaching the junction. The uterine artery is coagulated multiple times without cutting, ensuring hemostasis.

The surgeon approaches the opposite side of the uterine artery, applying coagulation laterally and medially without cutting. The uterine artery on the right side is visible during this process. The surgeon prepares for a second attempt where cutting will occur after initial coagulation.

The uterus appears paler, indicating cessation of blood supply. The surgeon performs final coagulation on the left side, ensuring the tissue is adequately irrigated to prevent overcooking. The uterine artery is visibly coagulated, and the eschar from the ligature is cleaned.

The surgeon cuts the left uterine pedicle and separates the cervicovaginal fascia using blunt dissection. The macon rod is also separated, and the posterior veins are coagulated to ensure complete detachment. The tip of the uterine structure is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon draws and cuts any remaining attachments on the left side, then pushes the uterus towards the contralateral shoulder to access the right side for further dissection.

The surgeon applies saline and betadine over the jaws of the ligature device before performing the second attempt to coagulate and cut the uterine artery. The uterine is then carefully manipulated posteriorly to ensure complete separation, addressing any remaining attachments.

The surgeon completes the coagulation and cutting of the uterine artery, resulting in the uterus appearing completely pale due to the cessation of blood supply. The colpotomy is prepared using a colpotomizer of appropriate size, either 3.5 or 4 centimeters. The harmonic scalpel is used to cut, with the assistant pushing the colpotomizer firmly to facilitate the incision.

The surgeon uses the vibrating tip of the harmonic scalpel to continue the colpotomy, cutting through the tissue with precision. The laparoscopic view shows the harmonic scalpel in action, while alternative instruments such as bipolar or monopolar devices are mentioned as options for performing the colpotomy if the harmonic is unavailable.

The surgeon completes the anterior and left side of the colpotomy, moving to the posterior part. The uterosacral ligament is preserved, particularly the vaginal portion, while separating tissue over the arc of the ligament. The surgical team coordinates the uterine manipulator and instruments to avoid overshooting during the laparoscopic hysterectomy.
The surgeon completes the final cut, freeing the uterus. The uterus is now visible as it is pulled through the vagina using the colpotomizer.

The surgeon applies anteromedial traction to the fallopian tube, separating it from the ovary. The tube is then pushed into the vagina. The process is repeated for the other tube, with the surgeon holding it and applying anteromedial traction. The infundibular ovarian ligament is grasped with a ligature device.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers near the fallopian tube to avoid collateral damage to the ovary and artery, ensuring that the ovary remains vascularized and preventing unintended coagulation.

The surgeon begins suturing the vaginal vault, taking sequential bites through the uterus, uterosacral ligament, and anterior and posterior vaginal walls. The assistant lifts the suture tail while the surgeon uses the left hand to support the suture and apply traction, and the right hand to continue taking bites. The suturing includes the uterosacral ligament, forming a double-layer closure with a second row of sutures near the tail end.

The surgeon completes the final knot for the vault closure, ensuring inclusion of the anterior and posterior vaginal fascia and epithelium to prevent granulation tissue formation. Various knot techniques are used, including extracorporeal square knot, surgeon's knot, Dundee jamming knot, continuous suturing, tumble square knot, and western knot.

The surgeon performs intracorporeal suturing to close the vaginal vault, starting from the right uterosacral ligament and continuing to the left side, then returning to approximate the angles. The final knot is tied using a surgeon's knot, ensuring careful handling of the suture near the needle to avoid pricking the iliac vessel or bowel.
